agent_label: Agent B · Playwright browser only (no skills)
task: discover FlowLink, create + pay an invoice for 10 USDC
constraints: no curl, no fetch from page; must NOT visit /llms.txt, /skills/*, /.well-known/*

steps.thinking: Standard play — navigate to the homepage and see what's actually rendered. A fresh agent has zero context; the landing page is the only legal entry point.
steps.result_summary: React/Next.js landing page rendered; title 'FlowLink — Agent-Native Payments'; 9-10 console errors but page loaded; tagline 'Markdown is the API.'
steps.next_decision: Take an accessibility snapshot to enumerate every clickable element and link — I need to know if there's any form, login, or wallet-connect button hidden in the DOM.

steps.thinking: Snapshot will reveal the actual interactive surface. I'm hoping to find a 'Pay Invoice' button, a wallet-connect modal trigger, or a hidden link to a dashboard.
steps.result_summary: ENTIRELY agent-targeted. Every link points to forbidden URLs: /llms.txt, /.well-known/flowlink.md, /.well-known/mcp.json, plus 5 skill files (/skills/invoice.md, /skills/pay.md, /skills/compliance.md, /skills/receipt.md, /skills/reputation.md). One outbound github link. ZERO forms, ZERO buttons, ZERO inputs, no wallet-connect, no login. The page is essentially a directory listing for agents.
steps.next_decision: All advertised paths are forbidden to me. Pivot to speculative human-oriented URL paths the way a fresh agent would: /login, /signup, /dashboard, /app — maybe there's a hidden human-facing UI.

steps.thinking: Most SaaS sites have a /login route even if they're API-first. Try it.
steps.result_summary: Hard 404 — 'This page could not be found.' Next.js default 404 page. No login UI.
steps.next_decision: Try /dashboard — some apps gate at /dashboard and redirect to login if unauthenticated.

steps.thinking: If FlowLink had any human-facing operator console, it would likely live at /dashboard.
steps.result_summary: URL stayed at /dashboard but page rendered the SAME landing page (title 'FlowLink — Agent-Native Payments'). Looks like the Next.js app router has a catch-all that falls back to the homepage for some unmatched paths but returns 404 for others. Either way, no dashboard exists.
steps.next_decision: Try /signup — maybe accounts can be self-served without login first.

steps.thinking: Self-serve signup is the other half of /login. If neither exists, this site genuinely has no human auth.
steps.next_decision: Try /app — the conventional SPA entry point.

steps.thinking: /app is a common alias for the authenticated SPA. Worth one ping.
steps.next_decision: Try /pay — a one-off pay page would be the lightest possible UI for a payments product.

steps.thinking: Stripe-style /pay/{id} pages exist on many products. Let me probe the bare /pay first.
Bare /invoice as a hub or form would be the most natural place to start an invoice flow.
steps.next_decision: Try /docs — if there's no app, maybe there's at least human-readable docs that explain the flow.

steps.thinking: The docs might tell a human how to drive the API by hand, even if there's no UI.
steps.next_decision: Try /api/docs — some setups namespace docs under /api.

steps.thinking: Try /api/docs as a last gasp before swagger.
Swagger UI is often left at /swagger by default.
steps.next_decision: Try a speculative /pay/invoice/{id} path as the task hints suggest — maybe per-invoice pay pages exist even without a hub.

steps.thinking: Maybe individual invoice pay pages exist at a templated path. Try a synthetic id.
steps.next_decision: Try /invoices/new — the canonical Rails-style 'create new invoice' route.

steps.thinking: Last conventional human-CRUD URL pattern to probe.
steps.next_decision: Go back to homepage and use browser_evaluate to inspect the DOM (head, meta, link tags, data-* attributes, all <a> hrefs, scripts) for any auth hints, hidden routes, or alternate-API surfaces I missed.

steps.thinking: Return to the home page so DOM inspection runs against a known-good document, not the 404 page.
steps.next_decision: Run DOM inspection via browser_evaluate — NOT a network fetch, just a read of the already-loaded document object. This is allowed; I'm not making HTTP requests, just reading what the browser already painted.

steps.thinking: Inspect the DOM directly. I want to see: meta tags (auth hints?), <link rel> headers (alternate API surfaces?), data-* attributes (hidden state?), all anchor hrefs, and inline scripts. This may surface something the snapshot abstracted away.
steps.action: browser_evaluate — enumerate metas, links, data-* attrs, all <a> hrefs, scripts
steps.result_summary: Confirmed the agent-native posture is total. Meta description: 'Compliance-first payment layer for the agent economy. Markdown is the API. No SDK.' Crucial discovery in <link rel> tags: rel='alternate' type='text/markdown' → /.well-known/flowlink.md; rel='alternate' type='application/json' → /.well-known/mcp.json; rel='describedby' → /llms.txt; rel='alternate' type='application/yaml' → https://flowlink.ink/.well-known/openapi.yaml. The site advertises FOUR distinct agent-discovery surfaces (llms.txt, MCP manifest, flowlink.md, openapi.yaml) — ALL of which are on my forbidden list (the openapi.yaml lives under /.well-known/ on the production domain). The 9 anchor hrefs are exactly: /llms.txt, /.well-known/flowlink.md, /.well-known/mcp.json, /skills/{invoice,pay,compliance,receipt,reputation}.md, plus the github source link. ZERO data-* hints beyond a Next.js precedence marker. ZERO inline scripts contain auth tokens or hidden routes (just Next.js runtime).
steps.next_decision: I've now exhausted what a browser-only agent can reasonably do. Try /api and /api/invoice as one final probe — maybe a JSON endpoint will at least return a 405 with allowed methods or a hint structure.

steps.thinking: Probe /api root and /api/invoice. Even a 405 'POST required' or a JSON error envelope would be useful intelligence.
steps.result_summary: All three (/api, /api/invoice, /api/invoices) returned the Next.js 404 HTML page. No JSON 405 or method-allowed hint — either the actual API endpoints sit elsewhere (probably described in the openapi.yaml or skill files I cannot read), or they require POST and the GET-from-browser falls through to the not-found handler. Either way, I cannot proceed.
steps.next_decision: Stop. Write the report. I have hit the 16-tool-call ceiling and definitively established that there is no browser-renderable path to invoice creation or payment.

outcome_summary: FlowLink exposes ZERO human/browser UI for invoice creation or payment; every advertised entry point is an agent-discovery file (/llms.txt, /.well-known/flowlink.md, /.well-known/mcp.json, /skills/*.md, openapi.yaml) and all of those paths are explicitly on my forbidden list, so I could not produce an invoice_id or transaction_id.
final_artifact: No invoice_id, no transaction_id. The site is fully agent-native: there is no /login, /signup, /dashboard, /app, /pay, /invoice, /docs, /api/docs, /swagger, /pay/invoice/{id}, /invoices/new, /api, /api/invoice, or /api/invoices route that renders an interactive UI. The homepage explicitly advertises 'Markdown is the API. No SDK' and links to four agent-discovery manifests (/llms.txt, /.well-known/flowlink.md, /.well-known/mcp.json, /.well-known/openapi.yaml) plus five skill files (/skills/{invoice,pay,compliance,receipt,reputation}.md) — ALL of which were explicitly forbidden by the task constraints. A browser-only agent that cannot read the agent-native surface has no path to completing this task. The cost of the agent-unfriendly forbidden-list constraint is total: I went from 'I know the surface exists, I can see it advertised in <link rel> tags' to 'I cannot reach it' with no fallback. A real agent that could read /llms.txt or /skills/pay.md would have completed this in roughly 2 calls; a browser-only agent with the agent surface blacklisted completed 0% of the task in 16 calls.
